fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out that component enhancement toolkit we've been talking about. The idea is basically that devs shouldn't have to write boilerplate wrapper components every time they want to do something simple like inject a prop, rename a key, or hold some local state. We want a composable pipeline approach — similar to what we did with the auth flow transformers last quarter, just refer to that pattern for how we chain things together.

The toolkit should handle property reshaping (things like overriding values, providing fallbacks, keeping/dropping specific keys, moving values around, spreading nested objects, and computing new values from existing ones). It also needs to cover local state management so components can track values and respond to updates without being rewritten. On top of that we want conditional rendering behavior, nesting helpers, re-render optimization, and some kind of event broadcasting utility.

One thing that kept coming up in the retro: when developers misuse the handler wiring incorrectly, they get cryptic runtime crashes instead of a clear message. That needs to be a proper normalized failure mode.

The whole thing must be broken into focused modules — no monolithic files. Each concern lives in its own unit. We also want the composition entry point to support chaining any number of enhancers cleanly. Output formatting for the test harness should emit sorted key=value lines with JSON-literal values. Coordinate with backend on the reducer pattern — they already have a counter schema we should reuse.

Quick follow-up from the questions that came in: the composition behavior should match the usual right-to-left flow. The rightmost function in the list is applied first, and its output feeds the next one to the left. If there are no functions, the seed just passes through unchanged. Also, that seed is provided as an argument list, so if the rightmost transformer takes multiple arguments, it can consume several seed values there. For the composition core specifically, the compose utility is a right-to-left function composer that takes an ordered list of enhancers (Component => Component functions) and returns a single composed enhancer, matching the signature compose(...enhancers)(BaseComponent).

For labels, the precedence should be explicit and predictable: explicit display label wins first, then the declared type name, then for an unnamed/anonymous inline component the literal string 'Component' is used as the fallback, and a raw markup tag string (like 'div') is returned verbatim.

A couple specifics on the branching pieces too. The branch_transform enhancer checks whether a named property is truthy. If it is, the when_true transformation (a merge descriptor) gets applied to the props; if not, the when_false transformation gets applied instead. Important part: the test property itself stays in the output alongside the merged result. And for branch_render, if when_false or when_true is set to 'render_nothing', the rendered output should be the literal string 'null' on a single line, which is how we represent an empty/null render rather than a markup tag.

On render output, the tree should print as an indented node listing. Each nesting level is indented by two spaces relative to its parent. Every line starts with the tag name followed by its props as key=value pairs, and the same props are passed to every level. If there are children, they show up as a plain string at the innermost level.

For memoization, we’ve got two strategies. The 'all' strategy compares every property shallowly and skips re-rendering when nothing has changed. The 'keys' strategy compares only the named subset of properties, so changes to anything else are ignored. The render count only increments when a watched property changes, and output should emit the cumulative render count after the initial render and after each property update.

For the event stream piece, think of it as pairing a push handler with an observable stream. Values pushed through the handler are delivered in order to every active subscriber, and each subscriber receives all emitted values. The output for that should list each subscriber's received sequence as subscriberN=[value1,value2,...] lines sorted by subscriber index.

Also, on the bad handler wiring case we called out before: when a handler factory does not return a function, meaning it is not a higher-order function, we should normalize that to 'invalid_handler_factory'. In output that should surface as error=invalid_handler_factory instead of exposing raw runtime exception details.

Last formatting note just to remove ambiguity: property output is emitted as key=value lines sorted alphabetically by key. Values are rendered as their JSON literals: strings are double-quoted, numbers are bare (no quotes), booleans are bare, and objects/arrays are compact JSON (no extra spaces). Each line ends with a newline character.

trigger_keywords: wrap display name format
how is the enhancer name combined with the component label
annotated label format
HoC label pattern
wrapper name display
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The wrapped label is produced by combining the enhancer name and the resolved component label in the form: wrapperName(label), for example someHoC(SomeComponent) or wrap(button).

trigger_keywords: merge vs defaults precedence
difference between merge and defaults
which one overrides incoming props
fallback only when missing
prop override direction
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: merge and defaults have opposite precedence. With merge, the fixed property map wins over any incoming value — collisions are resolved in favour of the merged values. With defaults, the incoming value is preserved if it exists; the fallback value only applies when the incoming property is missing or undefined.

trigger_keywords: flatten keeps original
does flatten remove the source property
original object after flatten
flatten retains parent key
spread nested object
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When flattening, the fields of the object-valued property are spread up to the top-level property set AND the original object property is also retained alongside the flattened fields. The keys parameter may be a single property name string or a list of names.

trigger_keywords: derive_join replaces all props
what happens to other props after derive
derive output set
only target key remains
derive_join source prop kept
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The derive_join operation replaces the entire incoming property set with a freshly computed one. Only the derived target key remains in the output — the source array property and all other incoming properties are dropped. The array is joined using the given separator and placed under the target key.

trigger_keywords: pipeline stage order
multiple stages direction
left to right prop transforms
later stage sees earlier output
stage sequencing
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When multiple transformation stages are listed, they are applied left to right. Each later stage observes the output of all earlier stages, not the original incoming properties.

trigger_keywords: stateful updater function types
what operations does the updater support
set vs add vs mul update
updater accepts replacement or function
state update kinds
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The state updater accepts either a direct replacement value (set: value) or a function of the previous value expressed as add: n (adds n to previous value) or mul: n (multiplies previous value by n). The initial state can be specified as a fixed seed via 'initial' or read from an incoming property via 'initial_from'.

trigger_keywords: reducer default state
what is the counter reducer initial value
reducer schema
reducer when no initial provided
default counter state
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The built-in counter reducer has a default initial state of { counter: 0 }. When no initial state is provided to the reducer_state command, the reducer's own default is used. The reducer handles a SET_COUNTER action whose payload becomes the new counter value. When initial_from is used, the named incoming property value is used as the initial counter.

trigger_keywords: state handlers merge partial
handler return value applied how
partial state update
handler returns nothing
shallowly merged handler output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Each named handler receives a payload and returns a partial state object that is shallowly merged into the current state. If a handler returns nothing (undefined), the state is left unchanged. The output emits the tracked state value after the initial render and after each handler call.

trigger_keywords: component_from_prop removes component key
which props forwarded after component_from_prop
prop_name property excluded from forwarded props
remaining props forwarded
tag selector prop dropped
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The component_from_prop enhancer reads the markup tag name from the designated property (prop_name), then renders that tag while forwarding all remaining properties to it. The property used to select the component (e.g., 'component') is NOT forwarded — only the other properties are passed to the rendered element.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

context_pointers.vague_reference: coordinate with backend on the reducer pattern — they already have a counter schema we should reuse
context_pointers.actual_meaning: The built-in counter reducer used in feature 4.2 (reducer_state command), which has a default initial state of { counter: 0 } and handles a SET_COUNTER action type whose payload becomes the new counter value.

The error category string 'invalid_handler_factory' emitted as error=invalid_handler_factory when a withHandlers-style factory does not return a function, as specified in feature 9.2 (handler_error command).

oracle_data.fallback_response: I don't have specific details on that aspect — could you check the existing codebase patterns or the test cases for the closest analogous behavior? If it's about sequencing or formatting, the general rule is: follow what the other similar enhancers do and keep each concern in its own module.

feature1_function_composition.json.description: Right-to-left composition of unary numeric transformers applied to a seed value; with no transformers the seed passes through unchanged, with one it is returned as-is, and the rightmost transformer runs first.

feature2_1_resolve_display_name.json.description: Resolve a component's human-facing label: an explicit label wins, otherwise the declared type name is used, an unnamed inline component falls back to the literal 'Component', and a raw markup tag string is returned verbatim.

feature2_2_wrap_display_name.json.description: Wrap a component's label with an enhancer name in the form 'wrapper(label)'.

feature3_1_merge_props.json.description: Merge a fixed set of properties onto the incoming properties; the merged values take precedence over incoming values with the same key. Output lists every resulting property as key=value lines sorted by key.

feature3_2_default_props.json.description: Supply fallback property values that apply only when the incoming property is missing or undefined; any defined incoming value is preserved.

feature3_3_pick_props.json.description: Keep only the named properties and drop the rest.

feature3_4_omit_props.json.description: Drop the named properties and keep the rest.

feature3_5_rename_prop.json.description: Rename one property to a new key, keeping its value and leaving other properties untouched.

feature3_6_rename_props.json.description: Rename several properties at once using an old-key to new-key map.

feature3_7_flatten_props.json.description: Spread the fields of one or more object-valued properties up into the top-level property set; the original object properties are also retained alongside the flattened fields.

feature3_8_derive_props.json.description: Replace the incoming property set with a freshly computed one derived from the inputs; here an array-valued property is joined into a single string under a new key.

feature3_9_pipeline_composition.json.description: Apply several property-transformation stages in sequence, left to right, so that later stages observe the output of earlier ones.

feature4_1_stateful_value.json.description: Attach a named state value and an updater. The updater accepts either a replacement value or a function of the previous value. State starts from a fixed seed or from an incoming property. Output emits the state after the initial render and after each update.

feature4_2_reducer_state.json.description: Attach a state value driven by a reducer plus a dispatch function; dispatched actions advance the state. Initial state may be a fixed object, derived from an incoming property, or supplied by the reducer's own default when omitted.

feature4_3_state_handlers.json.description: Attach a state object plus named updater handlers; each handler receives a payload and returns a partial state that is shallowly merged, or returns nothing to leave state unchanged. Output emits the tracked value after the initial render and after each call.

feature5_1_branch_transform.json.description: Test a property and apply one of two property transformations: when the tested property is truthy the first transformation runs, otherwise the second.

feature5_2_branch_render.json.description: Test a property and render one of two outcomes: a chosen markup tag, or nothing at all (an empty render). The rendered tree is printed as an indented node listing.

feature6_1_nest_components.json.description: Nest a series of markup tags from outer to inner, passing the same properties to each level and placing the supplied children at the innermost level. The rendered tree is printed as an indented node listing.

cases.expected_output:
div className="foo"
  section className="foo"
    span className="foo"
      Child

feature6_2_component_from_prop.json.description: Render whichever markup tag is named by a designated property, forwarding the remaining properties to it. The rendered tree is printed as an indented node listing.

feature7_render_memoization.json.description: Skip re-rendering when properties have not meaningfully changed. Two strategies exist: compare every property shallowly, or compare only a named subset of properties. Output emits the cumulative render count after the initial render and after each property update; the count only grows when a watched property changes.

feature8_1_event_stream.json.description: Create a push handler paired with an observable stream; values pushed through the handler are delivered in order to every active subscriber. Output lists each subscriber's received sequence.

feature9_1_cached_handlers.json.description: Expose stable handler functions derived from the current properties; a handler reads live property values and is recreated only when properties change. Output emits the value the handler reads at each render.

feature9_2_handler_error.json.description: When a supplied handler factory is not a higher-order function (it does not return a function), invoking the produced handler is reported as a normalized error category rather than leaking any runtime fault detail.
